flashcards.back: Open Systems Interconnection. It is a conceptual framework developed by ISO in 1984 that standardizes network communication functions into 7 abstract layers.

flashcards.front: List all 7 layers of the OSI model from top to bottom.
flashcards.back: Application (7), Presentation (6), Session (5), Transport (4), Network (3), Data Link (2), Physical (1). Mnemonic: All People Seem To Need Data Processing.

flashcards.front: What is the main function of the Application Layer (Layer 7)?
flashcards.back: It provides network services directly to end-user applications. It handles HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, Telnet protocols and enables software to access network resources.

flashcards.front: What is the role of the Presentation Layer (Layer 6)?
flashcards.back: It handles data translation, encryption, and compression. It ensures data from the Application Layer of one system can be understood by the Application Layer of another system (formatting, encoding).

flashcards.front: What does the Session Layer (Layer 5) do?
flashcards.back: It manages sessions (connections) between computers. It establishes, maintains, synchronizes, and terminates sessions between local and remote applications.

flashcards.front: What is the primary function of the Transport Layer (Layer 4)?
flashcards.back: It provides end-to-end communication, error-free delivery, flow control, and segmentation. It uses protocols like TCP (reliable) and UDP (unreliable).

flashcards.front: What are the responsibilities of the Network Layer (Layer 3)?
flashcards.back: It handles logical addressing (IP addresses) and routing. It determines the best path for data to travel from source to destination across multiple networks.

flashcards.front: What does the Data Link Layer (Layer 2) do?
flashcards.back: It provides node-to-node transfer, physical addressing (MAC addresses), error detection/correction, and frames data for transmission. It has LLC and MAC sub-layers.

flashcards.front: What is the function of the Physical Layer (Layer 1)?
flashcards.back: It deals with the physical connection and transmission of raw binary data (0s and 1s) over cables, wireless, or fiber. It defines hardware: cables, hubs, repeaters.

flashcards.front: What is encapsulation in the OSI model?
flashcards.back: The process where each layer adds its own header (and sometimes trailer) to the data as it passes down from Application to Physical layer. The data is wrapped with protocol information at each layer.

flashcards.front: What is de-encapsulation in the OSI model?
flashcards.back: The reverse process where each layer removes its corresponding header at the receiving side as data moves up from Physical to Application layer to retrieve the original data.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between TCP and UDP at Transport Layer?
flashcards.back: TCP provides reliable, connection-oriented delivery with error recovery and flow control. UDP provides unreliable, connectionless, faster delivery without guaranteed delivery or order.

flashcards.front: What are PDUs at each OSI layer?
flashcards.back: Application/Presentation/Session: Data; Transport: Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP); Network: Packet; Data Link: Frame; Physical: Bits.

flashcards.front: Give examples of protocols at each layer.
flashcards.back: Application: HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP; Presentation: JPEG, GIF, SSL, TLS; Session: RPC, NetBIOS; Transport: TCP, UDP; Network: IP, ICMP, IPX; Data Link: Ethernet, PPP, MAC; Physical: Ethernet cables, Fiber, Hub.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between logical and physical addressing?
flashcards.back: Logical addressing (IP) is assigned at Network Layer, can be changed, used for routing across networks. Physical addressing (MAC) is assigned at Data Link Layer, hardcoded in NIC, used for local network communication.
